1:
"When the LORD thy God shall bring thee into the land whither thou goest to possess it, and hath cast out many nations before thee... seven nations greater and mightier than thou;" The witnesses note God bringing Israel into the land and casting out seven nations greater than they. The witnesses note that Gill notes their introduction into the land is ascribed not to themselves or their leaders but to the Lord as their covenant God. The witnesses note that Henry marks the strict caution that follows against communion with the heathen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD shall bring thee — the conquest his work; the cast out many nations — seven greater than Israel; the not their own strength — the victory by his power; the a people kept his — the ground of the caution to follow.

The witnesses agree the conquest is owned from the first as God's work: it is "the LORD thy God" who brings them in and casts out the nations, greater and mightier than Israel, so that the victory rests not on their strength but on his power and covenant; the naming of seven nations stronger than themselves magnifies the grace that gives them the land, and the whole sets the ground for the strict caution to follow — that a people so brought in by God must keep themselves wholly his.

So the witnesses make the verse God brings them in: The witnesses note the the LORD shall bring thee: the conquest his work. The witnesses note the cast out many nations: seven greater than Israel. The witnesses note the not their own strength: the victory by his power. The witnesses note the a people kept his: the ground of the caution to follow. The expositors observe the the LORD shall bring thee: the conquest his work. The expositors observe the cast out many nations: seven greater than Israel. The expositors observe the not their own strength: the victory by his power. The expositors observe the a people kept his: the ground of the caution to follow. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD shall bring thee, the cast out many nations, the not their own strength, the a people kept his, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that God would bring Israel in and cast out seven greater nations — the conquest his work, the grace that gives the weaker the land, a people brought in by God to be kept wholly his.
2:
"And when the LORD thy God shall deliver them before thee; thou shalt smite them, and utterly destroy them; thou shalt make no covenant with them, nor shew mercy unto them:" The witnesses note the charge to utterly destroy the nations, making no covenant and showing no mercy. The witnesses note that Gill notes God would deliver them into Israel's hands. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the command — smite and utterly destroy them, make no covenant, show no mercy. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the deliver them before thee — into Israel's hand; the utterly destroy them — the righteous judgment; the no covenant — no league with the idolaters; the nor shew mercy — the cutting-off of a deadly contagion.

The witnesses agree the severity was a measure of judgment and a guard of holiness: the nations, ripe in their iniquity, were to be utterly destroyed, with no covenant and no sparing, lest any league with them draw Israel into their idolatry; the sternness, terrible to flesh, was God's righteous sentence on a corrupt people and his fence about his own — the "no mercy" not cruelty but the cutting-off of a deadly contagion, that the holy people not be ensnared by sparing the unholy.

So the witnesses make the verse utterly destroy: The witnesses note the deliver them before thee: into Israel's hand. The witnesses note the utterly destroy them: the righteous judgment. The witnesses note the no covenant: no league with the idolaters. The witnesses note the nor shew mercy: the cutting-off of a deadly contagion. The expositors observe the deliver them before thee: into Israel's hand. The expositors observe the utterly destroy them: the righteous judgment. The expositors observe the no covenant: no league with the idolaters. The expositors observe the nor shew mercy: the cutting-off of a deadly contagion. The believer is taught to mark the deliver them before thee, the utterly destroy them, the no covenant, the nor shew mercy, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that Israel was to utterly destroy the nations, making no covenant — God's righteous judgment on a corrupt people and a fence about his own, lest sparing the unholy ensnare the holy.
3:
"Neither shalt thou make marriages with them; thy daughter thou shalt not give unto his son, nor his daughter shalt thou take unto thy son." The witnesses note the forbidding of intermarriage with the heathen nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was unlawful unless they became proselytes (as Rahab, married by Salmon, and Ruth the Moabitess); the law's design was to prevent being drawn aside into idolatry. The witnesses note that bad consequences had followed such matches. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the make no marriages — with the heathen nations; the thy daughter, his son — no unions either way; the unless they turned — rahab and Ruth received as proselytes; the against idolatry, not race — marry only in the Lord (1 Cor. 7:39).

The witnesses agree the ban on intermarriage was a guard of the faith, not of race: marriage with the idolatrous nations was forbidden because of its peril to true worship, drawing the heart aside to false gods; yet, as Gill notes, those who turned to the LORD — a Rahab, a Ruth — were freely received and married, proving the bar was against the idolatry, not the nation; the principle stands for the church, that believers marry "only in the Lord" (1 Cor. 7:39), lest an unbelieving yoke draw the soul from God.

So the witnesses make the verse no intermarriage: The witnesses note the make no marriages: with the heathen nations. The witnesses note the thy daughter, his son: no unions either way. The witnesses note the unless they turned: Rahab and Ruth received as proselytes. The witnesses note the against idolatry, not race: marry only in the Lord (1 Cor. 7:39). The expositors observe the make no marriages: with the heathen nations. The expositors observe the thy daughter, his son: no unions either way. The expositors observe the unless they turned: Rahab and Ruth received as proselytes. The expositors observe the against idolatry, not race: marry only in the Lord (1 Cor. 7:39). The believer is taught to mark the make no marriages, the thy daughter, his son, the unless they turned, the against idolatry, not race, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that Israel was not to intermarry with the idolaters — the bar against the idolatry, not the nation (Rahab and Ruth received), a guard for the church to marry only in the Lord (1 Cor. 7:39).
4:
"For they will turn away thy son from following me, that they may serve other gods: so will the anger of the LORD be kindled against you, and destroy thee suddenly." The witnesses note the reason of the ban — such marriages would turn the heart to other gods and kindle God's anger. The witnesses note that Gill notes they would turn the son away from the pure worship of God, his word, statutes, and ordinances. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the manifest danger of apostasy and idolatry from such matches; the reason limits the law to the unconverted (else Salmon married Rahab, Matt. 1:5). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turn away thy son — from following the LORD; the to serve other gods — the peril of apostasy; the God's anger kindled — sudden destruction threatened; the limited to the unconverted — the peril, not the nation (Matt. 1:5).

The witnesses agree the danger named is the heart drawn from God: the idolatrous spouse would "turn away thy son from following me," and so provoke the LORD to anger and sudden destruction; Wesley marks that this very reason both limits the law to the unconverted and extends its principle wherever the like peril lies — the abiding lesson that an ungodly union endangers the soul and the seed, the influence of an idolatrous heart being a snare to faith, against which God jealously guards his people.

So the witnesses make the verse turning the heart: The witnesses note the turn away thy son: from following the LORD. The witnesses note the to serve other gods: the peril of apostasy. The witnesses note the God's anger kindled: sudden destruction threatened. The witnesses note the limited to the unconverted: the peril, not the nation (Matt. 1:5). The expositors observe the turn away thy son: from following the LORD. The expositors observe the to serve other gods: the peril of apostasy. The expositors observe the God's anger kindled: sudden destruction threatened. The expositors observe the limited to the unconverted: the peril, not the nation (Matt. 1:5). The believer is taught to mark the turn away thy son, the to serve other gods, the God's anger kindled, the limited to the unconverted, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that the marriages would turn the heart to other gods and kindle God's anger — the peril of an ungodly union to soul and seed, the law aimed at the idolatry, not the converted (Matt. 1:5).
5:
"But thus shall ye deal with them; ye shall destroy their altars, and break down their images, and cut down their groves, and burn their graven images with fire." The witnesses note the charge to destroy utterly the altars, images, and groves of the heathen worship. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was how Israel was to deal with the inhabitants of Canaan. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes that destroying the objects of their worship would force the inhabitants either to defend their gods, ending in their extermination, or to abandon them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the destroy their altars — the seats of false worship; the break their images — and cut down the groves; the burn the graven gods — with fire, utterly; the no truce with idolatry — every relic put away.

The witnesses agree the destruction of the idols was a thorough rooting-out of idolatry, leaving no relic to ensnare: the altars, images, groves, and graven gods of the nations were all to be broken and burned, that no trace of false worship remain in the land; the thoroughness teaches that the people of God are to make no truce with idolatry but to put it utterly away, sparing nothing of it — a zeal for the LORD's sole worship that tolerates no rival shrine, the holy land cleansed of every idol.

So the witnesses make the verse destroy the idols: The witnesses note the destroy their altars: the seats of false worship. The witnesses note the break their images: and cut down the groves. The witnesses note the burn the graven gods: with fire, utterly. The witnesses note the no truce with idolatry: every relic put away. The expositors observe the destroy their altars: the seats of false worship. The expositors observe the break their images: and cut down the groves. The expositors observe the burn the graven gods: with fire, utterly. The expositors observe the no truce with idolatry: every relic put away. The believer is taught to mark the destroy their altars, the break their images, the burn the graven gods, the no truce with idolatry, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that Israel was to destroy utterly the altars and idols of the nations — a thorough rooting-out of idolatry, the people of God making no truce with it but cleansing the land of every idol.
6:
"For thou art an holy people unto the LORD thy God: the LORD thy God hath chosen thee to be a special people unto himself, above all people that are upon the face of the earth." The witnesses note Israel a holy people, chosen by God to be his special treasure above all peoples. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were holy in the sense of being separated from all other people to the pure worship and service of God, and therefore were to avoid all idolatry. The witnesses note that God had chosen them to be a special people unto himself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an holy people — separated to God's worship; the the LORD hath chosen thee — his election of grace; the a special people — his treasure above all the earth; the the church's calling — a holy nation, a peculiar people (1 Pet. 2:9).

The witnesses agree the ground of all the foregoing commands is Israel's election and holiness: they were a people "chosen" and set apart, God's "special treasure" above all the earth, and therefore must keep themselves from the defilement of idolatry; the holiness here is chiefly that of separation — a people consecrated to God's worship — yet it points to the deeper calling of the church, "a chosen generation, a holy nation, a peculiar people" (1 Pet. 2:9), bound by their election to live wholly unto him who chose them.

So the witnesses make the verse a holy chosen people: The witnesses note the an holy people: separated to God's worship. The witnesses note the the LORD hath chosen thee: his election of grace. The witnesses note the a special people: his treasure above all the earth. The witnesses note the the church's calling: a holy nation, a peculiar people (1 Pet. 2:9). The expositors observe the an holy people: separated to God's worship. The expositors observe the the LORD hath chosen thee: his election of grace. The expositors observe the a special people: his treasure above all the earth. The expositors observe the the church's calling: a holy nation, a peculiar people (1 Pet. 2:9). The believer is taught to mark the an holy people, the the LORD hath chosen thee, the a special people, the the church's calling, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that Israel was a holy people, God's chosen treasure above all the earth — their election the ground of their separation, pointing to the church, a holy nation bound to live wholly unto him who chose them (1 Pet. 2:9).
7:
"The LORD did not set his love upon you, nor choose you, because ye were more in number than any people; for ye were the fewest of all people:" The witnesses note that God's love and choice rested not on Israel's greatness, for they were the fewest of all peoples. The witnesses note that Gill notes God had done both — loved them and chosen them, the one the effect and evidence of the other — but neither because of their number. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note they were the fewest of all peoples. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not set his love — for any merit in them; the nor choose you — for greatness; the the fewest of all — no ground in their number; the sovereign grace — that no flesh should glory (1 Cor. 1:27).

The witnesses agree the verse strikes at all merit in the creature: God did not love or choose Israel for anything in them — not their numbers, for they were the fewest — but of his own free will; the truth lays the axe to human pride and exalts sovereign grace, that election springs not from the worth or greatness of the chosen but from the good pleasure of God alone, who "hath chosen the weak things... that no flesh should glory in his presence" (1 Cor. 1:27).

So the witnesses make the verse not for their number: The witnesses note the not set his love: for any merit in them. The witnesses note the nor choose you: for greatness. The witnesses note the the fewest of all: no ground in their number. The witnesses note the sovereign grace: that no flesh should glory (1 Cor. 1:27). The expositors observe the not set his love: for any merit in them. The expositors observe the nor choose you: for greatness. The expositors observe the the fewest of all: no ground in their number. The expositors observe the sovereign grace: that no flesh should glory (1 Cor. 1:27). The believer is taught to mark the not set his love, the nor choose you, the the fewest of all, the sovereign grace, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that God chose Israel not for their number, for they were the fewest — election springing not from the worth of the chosen but from the free good pleasure of God, that no flesh should glory (1 Cor. 1:27).
8:
"But because the LORD loved you, and because he would keep the oath which he had sworn unto your fathers, hath the LORD brought you out... and redeemed you out of the house of bondmen..." The witnesses note the true ground of Israel's redemption — God's free love and his faithfulness to his oath. The witnesses note that Gill notes God loved them with an unmerited love — he loved them because he would love them, not for any goodness in them. The witnesses note that Clarke notes it was no good in them that induced God to choose them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the because the LORD loved you — love resting on his own will; the the oath to your fathers — his faithfulness to his word; the an unmerited love — for no goodness in them; the free, self-moved grace — not of him that willeth (Rom. 9:16).

The witnesses agree the reason of God's love is found in God alone: "because the LORD loved you" — a love resting on no cause but his own will, and on his faithfulness to the oath sworn to the fathers; the circular reason Gill draws out — "he loved them because he would love them" — is the deepest ground of grace, that God's love is free, self-moved, and unmerited, the spring of redemption being his own heart and his own word, not anything in the redeemed (cf. Rom. 9:16).

So the witnesses make the verse because he loved you: The witnesses note the because the LORD loved you: love resting on his own will. The witnesses note the the oath to your fathers: his faithfulness to his word. The witnesses note the an unmerited love: for no goodness in them. The witnesses note the free, self-moved grace: not of him that willeth (Rom. 9:16). The expositors observe the because the LORD loved you: love resting on his own will. The expositors observe the the oath to your fathers: his faithfulness to his word. The expositors observe the an unmerited love: for no goodness in them. The expositors observe the free, self-moved grace: not of him that willeth (Rom. 9:16). The believer is taught to mark the because the LORD loved you, the the oath to your fathers, the an unmerited love, the free, self-moved grace, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that God redeemed Israel because he loved them and kept his oath — a free, self-moved, unmerited love, the spring of redemption his own heart and word, not anything in the redeemed (Rom. 9:16).
9:
"Know therefore that the LORD thy God, he is God, the faithful God, which keepeth covenant and mercy with them that love him and keep his commandments to a thousand generations;" The witnesses note the call to know the LORD as the faithful God who keeps covenant to a thousand generations. The witnesses note that Gill notes he is the only true and living God, not the idols of the Gentiles, false and lifeless. The witnesses note that Calvin notes "thou shalt know" may be read in the future, referring to an experimental knowledge gained by proof. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD is God — the only true and living God; the the faithful God — keeping covenant and mercy; the to a thousand generations — his grace enduring all ages; the known by experience — his faithfulness proved (2 Tim. 2:13).

The witnesses agree the verse calls Israel to a settled and experimental knowledge of God's faithfulness: he is "the faithful God," who keeps covenant and mercy to "a thousand generations" of them that love him; the vast reach of the promise — a thousand generations — magnifies the constancy of his grace, that his covenant love does not fail but endures through all ages to his people; and the knowing of it, as Calvin marks, is to be learned by experience, the believer proving in his own life the unchanging faithfulness of God (cf. 2 Tim. 2:13).

So the witnesses make the verse the faithful God: The witnesses note the the LORD is God: the only true and living God. The witnesses note the the faithful God: keeping covenant and mercy. The witnesses note the to a thousand generations: his grace enduring all ages. The witnesses note the known by experience: his faithfulness proved (2 Tim. 2:13). The expositors observe the the LORD is God: the only true and living God. The expositors observe the the faithful God: keeping covenant and mercy. The expositors observe the to a thousand generations: his grace enduring all ages. The expositors observe the known by experience: his faithfulness proved (2 Tim. 2:13). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD is God, the the faithful God, the to a thousand generations, the known by experience, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that the LORD is the faithful God, keeping covenant to a thousand generations — the constancy of his grace through all ages, known by experience, his faithfulness never failing (2 Tim. 2:13).
10:
"And repayeth them that hate him to their face, to destroy them: he will not be slack to him that hateth him, he will repay him to his face." The witnesses note the other side of God's justice — he repays those who hate him, openly and without delay. The witnesses note that Gill notes he repays them that hate him to their face, openly and at once, and is not slack; Onkelos and the Targums read it "in their lifetime." The witnesses note that he will repay the audacious, bold sinner to his face. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the repayeth them that hate him — his justice on the impenitent; the to their face — openly and certainly; the not slack — no delay in the requital; the mercy and judgment — his justice as sure as his grace (Nah. 1:3).

The witnesses agree the faithfulness that keeps mercy to lovers of God is matched by the justice that repays his haters: God will not be slack but will requite the impenitent "to their face," openly and certainly; the joining of mercy and judgment shows the whole character of God — gracious to them that love him, terrible to them that hate him — so that none presume on his patience, for the same God who keeps covenant to a thousand generations will surely repay the enemy, his justice as sure as his grace (cf. Nah. 1:3).

So the witnesses make the verse repaying his haters: The witnesses note the repayeth them that hate him: his justice on the impenitent. The witnesses note the to their face: openly and certainly. The witnesses note the not slack: no delay in the requital. The witnesses note the mercy and judgment: his justice as sure as his grace (Nah. 1:3). The expositors observe the repayeth them that hate him: his justice on the impenitent. The expositors observe the to their face: openly and certainly. The expositors observe the not slack: no delay in the requital. The expositors observe the mercy and judgment: his justice as sure as his grace (Nah. 1:3). The believer is taught to mark the repayeth them that hate him, the to their face, the not slack, the mercy and judgment, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that God repays his haters to their face, not slack — his justice matching his mercy, the same God gracious to them that love him and terrible to his enemies, his judgment as sure as his grace (Nah. 1:3).
11:
"Thou shalt therefore keep the commandments, and the statutes, and the judgments, which I command thee this day, to do them." The witnesses note the charge drawn from all the foregoing — therefore keep and do the commandments. The witnesses note that Gill notes the laws — moral, ceremonial, and judicial — were to be kept, urged by both promises and threatenings, in hope of reward and fear of punishment. The witnesses note that they were to be done, not merely heard. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep the commandments — the practical conclusion; the statutes and judgments — the whole law; the to do them — obedience, not hearing only; the grace the spring — god's love moving to obey.

The witnesses agree the "therefore" binds obedience to all that has gone before — God's election, redemption, faithfulness, and justice: because the LORD is such a God, his people must keep his commandments; the practical conclusion shows that grace is the spring of obedience, not its rival — the knowledge of God's love and faithfulness moving the heart to keep his word, urged on by his promises and warnings alike, that the chosen people prove their calling in a life of doing his commandments.

So the witnesses make the verse therefore keep them: The witnesses note the keep the commandments: the practical conclusion. The witnesses note the statutes and judgments: the whole law. The witnesses note the to do them: obedience, not hearing only. The witnesses note the grace the spring: God's love moving to obey. The expositors observe the keep the commandments: the practical conclusion. The expositors observe the statutes and judgments: the whole law. The expositors observe the to do them: obedience, not hearing only. The expositors observe the grace the spring: God's love moving to obey. The believer is taught to mark the keep the commandments, the statutes and judgments, the to do them, the grace the spring, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that Israel was therefore to keep and do the commandments — grace the spring of obedience, the knowledge of God's love and faithfulness moving the chosen people to a life of doing his word.
12:
"Wherefore it shall come to pass, if ye hearken to these judgments, and keep, and do them, that the LORD thy God shall keep unto thee the covenant and the mercy which he sware unto thy fathers:" The witnesses note the promise — if they keep his judgments, God will keep his covenant and mercy sworn to the fathers. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were attentively to listen to the judgments and be careful to observe them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the promise — the LORD would keep the covenant and mercy he sware unto the fathers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if ye hearken — attentive obedience; the keep and do them — careful observance; the the LORD shall keep — the covenant and mercy; the the way of blessing — grace enjoyed in obedience.

The witnesses agree the verse joins their obedience to God's covenant-keeping: as they hearkened and kept his judgments, so God would keep to them the covenant and mercy sworn to the fathers; the mutual relation — not that their obedience earned the covenant, but that it was the appointed way of enjoying its blessings — shows the order of grace, that God's faithful keeping of his promise is experienced in the path of obedience, the covenant mercies flowing to a people that walks in his ways.

So the witnesses make the verse the covenant kept: The witnesses note the if ye hearken: attentive obedience. The witnesses note the keep and do them: careful observance. The witnesses note the the LORD shall keep: the covenant and mercy. The witnesses note the the way of blessing: grace enjoyed in obedience. The expositors observe the if ye hearken: attentive obedience. The expositors observe the keep and do them: careful observance. The expositors observe the the LORD shall keep: the covenant and mercy. The expositors observe the the way of blessing: grace enjoyed in obedience. The believer is taught to mark the if ye hearken, the keep and do them, the the LORD shall keep, the the way of blessing, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that if they kept his judgments, God would keep the covenant sworn to the fathers — obedience the appointed way of enjoying the covenant blessings, grace experienced in the path of his ways.
13:
"And he will love thee, and bless thee, and multiply thee: he will also bless the fruit of thy womb, and the fruit of thy land, thy corn, and thy wine, and thine oil..." The witnesses note the rich covenant blessings promised — love, increase, and the fruitfulness of womb and land. The witnesses note that Gill notes God will love them, as he has done, and rest in his love, giving further proofs of it. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the blessing of the fruit of the womb, the land, the corn, wine, oil, and flocks. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he will love thee — his abiding love; the bless and multiply — increase of the people; the fruit of womb and land — family, field, and flock; the the fountain of good — his love pouring out all blessing (Eph. 1:3).

The witnesses agree the blessings flow from God's abiding love: he would "love thee, and bless thee, and multiply thee," crowning his people with increase of family, field, and flock; the heaping-up of blessings — womb, land, corn, wine, oil, herds — shows the lavishness of God's goodness to them that keep his covenant, his love not a bare sentiment but a fountain of good gifts, resting on his people and pouring out upon them all the fruits of his favour (cf. Eph. 1:3).

So the witnesses make the verse the covenant blessing: The witnesses note the he will love thee: his abiding love. The witnesses note the bless and multiply: increase of the people. The witnesses note the fruit of womb and land: family, field, and flock. The witnesses note the the fountain of good: his love pouring out all blessing (Eph. 1:3). The expositors observe the he will love thee: his abiding love. The expositors observe the bless and multiply: increase of the people. The expositors observe the fruit of womb and land: family, field, and flock. The expositors observe the the fountain of good: his love pouring out all blessing (Eph. 1:3). The believer is taught to mark the he will love thee, the bless and multiply, the fruit of womb and land, the the fountain of good, and to rest in the electing love of God. The verse thus records that God would love, bless, and multiply his people, blessing womb and land — the lavishness of his goodness to them that keep his covenant, his love a fountain of all good gifts (Eph. 1:3).
14:
"Thou shalt be blessed above all people: there shall not be male or female barren among you, or among your cattle." The witnesses note the promise of blessing above all peoples, with fruitfulness and no barrenness. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were blessed above all people even with temporal blessings, besides the religious — the oracles, covenants, law, and promises. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the promise of no barrenness among them or their cattle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the blessed above all people — in things temporal and spiritual; the no barren among you — fruitful in family; the nor among your cattle — fruitful in flock; the God's favour on all — the covenant blessing reaching every part.

The witnesses agree the promise crowns the covenant blessing with fruitfulness: Israel should be blessed above all peoples, with neither man nor beast barren among them, a token of God's favour resting on every part of their life; the abundance — fruitful in family and flock — shows that the God who chose them would prosper them, the blessings of the covenant reaching to the very increase of their households, a people made fruitful by the favour of their God.

So the witnesses make the verse blessed above all: The witnesses note the blessed above all people: in things temporal and spiritual. The witnesses note the no barren among you: fruitful in family. The witnesses note the nor among your cattle: fruitful in flock. The witnesses note the God's favour on all: the covenant blessing reaching every part. The expositors observe the blessed above all people: in things temporal and spiritual. The expositors observe the no barren among you: fruitful in family. The expositors observe the nor among your cattle: fruitful in flock. The expositors observe the God's favour on all: the covenant blessing reaching every part. The believer is taught to mark the blessed above all people, the no barren among you, the nor among your cattle, the God's favour on all, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that Israel should be blessed above all peoples, with no barrenness — God's favour resting on every part of their life, the covenant blessing reaching to the increase of household and flock.
15:
"And the LORD will take away from thee all sickness, and will put none of the evil diseases of Egypt... upon thee; but will lay them upon all them that hate thee." The witnesses note the promise of health — God removing sickness and the evil diseases of Egypt from his people. The witnesses note that Gill notes God would take away bodily sickness, prevent it coming, or remove it when come. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the word for "diseases" is found only in Deuteronomy, the law being in many details a sanitary as well as a moral code. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the take away all sickness — god removing disease; the none of Egypt's diseases — sparing his people the plagues; the the law a sanitary code — caring for the body's welfare; the laid on their haters — the LORD the keeper of their health (Exod. 15:26).

The witnesses agree the promise of health is part of the covenant favour, and a token of God's care for the whole man: he would take away sickness and spare them the diseases of Egypt, the law itself, as Ellicott marks, providing for the body's welfare as well as the soul's; the laying of those plagues instead on Israel's enemies shows the same hand that blesses the faithful chastising the hostile, the LORD the keeper of his people's health as of all their good (cf. Exod. 15:26).

So the witnesses make the verse health promised: The witnesses note the take away all sickness: God removing disease. The witnesses note the none of Egypt's diseases: sparing his people the plagues. The witnesses note the the law a sanitary code: caring for the body's welfare. The witnesses note the laid on their haters: the LORD the keeper of their health (Exod. 15:26). The expositors observe the take away all sickness: God removing disease. The expositors observe the none of Egypt's diseases: sparing his people the plagues. The expositors observe the the law a sanitary code: caring for the body's welfare. The expositors observe the laid on their haters: the LORD the keeper of their health (Exod. 15:26). The believer is taught to mark the take away all sickness, the none of Egypt's diseases, the the law a sanitary code, the laid on their haters, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that God would take away sickness and spare them Egypt's diseases — his care for the whole man, the LORD the keeper of his people's health, the plagues laid instead on their haters (Exod. 15:26).
16:
"And thou shalt consume all the people which the LORD thy God shall deliver thee; thine eye shall have no pity upon them: neither shalt thou serve their gods; for that will be a snare unto thee." The witnesses note the charge to consume the nations without pity, and not to serve their gods, which would be a snare. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not to spare but utterly destroy the inhabitants God delivered to them. The witnesses note that Ellicott renders "consume" literally as "eat up" all the people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the consume all the people — no sparing the nations; the no pity — lest sparing lead to snaring; the serve not their gods — no truce with idolatry; the a snare to the soul — toleration catching the heart (Ps. 106:36).

The witnesses agree the command joins the destruction of the nations to the great reason behind it — that their gods would be a snare: Israel was to show no pity, lest sparing the people lead to sparing their idolatry, which would entangle the soul; the "snare" names the deadly peril of toleration, that any league with idolatry catches and holds the heart, so that the severity was mercy to Israel's own faith — the cutting-off of a temptation that would otherwise ensnare them (cf. Ps. 106:36, their idols which were a snare).

So the witnesses make the verse a snare to avoid: The witnesses note the consume all the people: no sparing the nations. The witnesses note the no pity: lest sparing lead to snaring. The witnesses note the serve not their gods: no truce with idolatry. The witnesses note the a snare to the soul: toleration catching the heart (Ps. 106:36). The expositors observe the consume all the people: no sparing the nations. The expositors observe the no pity: lest sparing lead to snaring. The expositors observe the serve not their gods: no truce with idolatry. The expositors observe the a snare to the soul: toleration catching the heart (Ps. 106:36). The believer is taught to mark the consume all the people, the no pity, the serve not their gods, the a snare to the soul, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that Israel was to consume the nations without pity and not serve their gods, which would be a snare — the severity a mercy to their own faith, cutting off the idolatry that would ensnare the heart (Ps. 106:36).
17:
"If thou shalt say in thine heart, These nations are more than I; how can I dispossess them?" The witnesses note the secret fear that might arise — the nations too many to dispossess. The witnesses note that Gill notes these might be secret thoughts arising in the heart, misgivings, fears, and doubts inwardly retained though not outwardly expressed. The witnesses note that Calvin notes it was a matter of great difficulty to destroy so great a multitude, and despair might drive them to madness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the say in thine heart — the inward misgiving; the these nations are more — the foe greater than they; the how can I dispossess — the doubt of unbelief; the God forestalls it — meeting the heart's fear with his word.

The witnesses agree the verse meets the unbelief that whispers in the heart: faced with nations more and mightier than themselves, Israel might secretly doubt how they could ever dispossess them; Calvin marks the real difficulty that bred such fear, the multitude of the foe enough to drive the faithless to despair — yet God anticipates the doubt to answer it, showing his tender care to forestall the inward fears of his people and to meet the secret misgivings of the heart with his sure word.

So the witnesses make the verse the secret fear: The witnesses note the say in thine heart: the inward misgiving. The witnesses note the these nations are more: the foe greater than they. The witnesses note the how can I dispossess: the doubt of unbelief. The witnesses note the God forestalls it: meeting the heart's fear with his word. The expositors observe the say in thine heart: the inward misgiving. The expositors observe the these nations are more: the foe greater than they. The expositors observe the how can I dispossess: the doubt of unbelief. The expositors observe the God forestalls it: meeting the heart's fear with his word. The believer is taught to mark the say in thine heart, the these nations are more, the how can I dispossess, the God forestalls it, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that Israel might secretly fear the nations too many to dispossess — the inward misgiving of unbelief, which God anticipates to answer, meeting the secret fears of the heart with his sure word.
18:
"Thou shalt not be afraid of them: but shalt well remember what the LORD thy God did unto Pharaoh, and unto all Egypt;" The witnesses note the cure for fear — to remember well what God did to Pharaoh and Egypt. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not to be afraid on account of the nations' number or strength. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the remedy — well remember what the LORD did unto Pharaoh and all Egypt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the be not afraid — against the dread of the nations; the well remember — the cure for fear; the what God did to Pharaoh — a greater power overthrown; the faith fed by memory — past mercy answering present fear (1 Sam. 17:37).

The witnesses agree the antidote to fear is remembrance: against the dread of mighty nations, Israel was to "well remember" how God had overthrown Pharaoh and all Egypt, a greater power than these; the calling-up of past deliverance is the appointed cure for present fear, the believer bidden to meet new trials by recalling old mercies, reasoning from what God has done to what he will do, faith fed by memory until fear is cast out (cf. 1 Sam. 17:37).

So the witnesses make the verse remember Egypt: The witnesses note the be not afraid: against the dread of the nations. The witnesses note the well remember: the cure for fear. The witnesses note the what God did to Pharaoh: a greater power overthrown. The witnesses note the faith fed by memory: past mercy answering present fear (1 Sam. 17:37). The expositors observe the be not afraid: against the dread of the nations. The expositors observe the well remember: the cure for fear. The expositors observe the what God did to Pharaoh: a greater power overthrown. The expositors observe the faith fed by memory: past mercy answering present fear (1 Sam. 17:37). The believer is taught to mark the be not afraid, the well remember, the what God did to Pharaoh, the faith fed by memory, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that Israel was not to fear but to remember God's overthrow of Pharaoh — remembrance the cure for fear, faith fed by memory, reasoning from what God has done to what he will do (1 Sam. 17:37).
19:
"The great temptations which thine eyes saw, and the signs, and the wonders, and the mighty hand, and the stretched out arm... so shall the LORD thy God do unto all the people of whom thou art afraid." The witnesses note the great works in Egypt, seen with their own eyes, as the pledge of what God would do to all they feared. The witnesses note that Gill notes the miracles wrought in Egypt. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the "temptations" were the repeated summons to Pharaoh, enforced by plagues — trials of his character. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the signs and wonders — seen with their own eyes; the the mighty hand — god's power in Egypt; the so shall the LORD do — to all they feared; the the past a pledge — the stretched-out arm still for them.

The witnesses agree the past wonders are the sure pledge of future victory: the very eyes that had seen the signs and the mighty hand in Egypt were the warrant that "so shall the LORD do" to all the nations they feared; the argument from experience — God who did this will do that — is the ground of confident faith, the believer reasoning from the proven power of God in former deliverances to his certain help in present need, the stretched-out arm that smote Egypt stretched still for his people.

So the witnesses make the verse so shall the LORD do: The witnesses note the the signs and wonders: seen with their own eyes. The witnesses note the the mighty hand: God's power in Egypt. The witnesses note the so shall the LORD do: to all they feared. The witnesses note the the past a pledge: the stretched-out arm still for them. The expositors observe the the signs and wonders: seen with their own eyes. The expositors observe the the mighty hand: God's power in Egypt. The expositors observe the so shall the LORD do: to all they feared. The expositors observe the the past a pledge: the stretched-out arm still for them. The believer is taught to mark the the signs and wonders, the the mighty hand, the so shall the LORD do, the the past a pledge, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that the great works seen in Egypt were the pledge that so God would do to all they feared — the proven power of God in former deliverance the ground of confidence for present need, the stretched-out arm still for his people.
20:
"Moreover the LORD thy God will send the hornet among them, until they that are left, and hide themselves from thee, be destroyed." The witnesses note God sending the hornet to drive out and destroy even those who hid themselves. The witnesses note that Gill notes the hornet, a bold and venomous insect, twice as large as a wasp, sent among them; Aben Ezra reads it of the leprosy. The witnesses note that Ellicott takes it literally. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the send the hornet — the least creature his instrument; the among them — to rout the nations; the that hide themselves — the hidden flushed out; the all means his — no enemy escaping God (Exod. 23:28).

The witnesses agree the hornet shows God using even the least of creatures to accomplish his purpose: he would send these to rout the nations and to ferret out those who hid themselves, finishing the work no human sword could reach; the lesson is that God has all means at his command, the smallest creature an instrument of his will, so that no enemy can escape him — the hidden flushed out and destroyed by a power that needs no army, God's victory complete by whatever means he chooses (cf. Exod. 23:28).

So the witnesses make the verse the hornet sent: The witnesses note the send the hornet: the least creature his instrument. The witnesses note the among them: to rout the nations. The witnesses note the that hide themselves: the hidden flushed out. The witnesses note the all means his: no enemy escaping God (Exod. 23:28). The expositors observe the send the hornet: the least creature his instrument. The expositors observe the among them: to rout the nations. The expositors observe the that hide themselves: the hidden flushed out. The expositors observe the all means his: no enemy escaping God (Exod. 23:28). The believer is taught to mark the send the hornet, the among them, the that hide themselves, the all means his, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that God would send the hornet to destroy even those who hid — the least creature his instrument, no enemy escaping him, his victory complete by whatever means he chooses (Exod. 23:28).
21:
"Thou shalt not be affrighted at them: for the LORD thy God is among you, a mighty God and terrible." The witnesses note the ground of courage — the LORD, a mighty and terrible God, is in the midst of his people. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not to be affrighted at the nations' numbers or gigantic stature. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Israel had no need to fear, as Jehovah was in the midst of it, a mighty God and terrible, who would drive out the nations. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the be not affrighted — against the dread of giants; the the LORD is among you — his presence in the midst; the a mighty God and terrible — terrible to his enemies; the the cure for fear — greater is he that is in you (1 John 4:4).

The witnesses agree the presence of God in the midst is the cure for every terror: Israel need not be affrighted at the giants or their numbers, "for the LORD thy God is among you, a mighty God and terrible"; the indwelling presence of the Almighty turns the fear of the foe into confidence, for the God who is terrible to his enemies is the shield of his people — the same truth that steadies the believer, that "greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world" (1 John 4:4).

So the witnesses make the verse God in the midst: The witnesses note the be not affrighted: against the dread of giants. The witnesses note the the LORD is among you: his presence in the midst. The witnesses note the a mighty God and terrible: terrible to his enemies. The witnesses note the the cure for fear: greater is he that is in you (1 John 4:4). The expositors observe the be not affrighted: against the dread of giants. The expositors observe the the LORD is among you: his presence in the midst. The expositors observe the a mighty God and terrible: terrible to his enemies. The expositors observe the the cure for fear: greater is he that is in you (1 John 4:4). The believer is taught to mark the be not affrighted, the the LORD is among you, the a mighty God and terrible, the the cure for fear, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that Israel need not be affrighted, for the mighty and terrible God was in their midst — his indwelling presence the cure for every terror, greater is he that is in you than he in the world (1 John 4:4).
22:
"And the LORD thy God will put out those nations before thee by little and little: thou mayest not consume them at once, lest the beasts of the field increase upon thee." The witnesses note that God would drive out the nations gradually, lest the wild beasts multiply in a sudden-emptied land. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was observed for their encouragement, lest seeing all not destroyed at once they fear the work would never be accomplished. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the gradual driving-out, lest the beasts of the field increase. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the put out by little and little — the gradual conquest; the not consume at once — lest the land lie empty; the lest the beasts increase — a danger forestalled; the God's wise gradual working — deliverance by degrees, fitted to their strength.

The witnesses agree the gradual conquest was an act of God's wisdom and care: he would drive out the nations "by little and little," not all at once, lest the land lie empty and the wild beasts multiply to Israel's harm; the slowness, which might have bred doubt, was in truth a mercy fitted to their strength, teaching that God's deliverances often come by degrees, and that his gradual working — in providence and in the soul's sanctification — is wise and kind, giving his people no more at once than they can rightly possess.

So the witnesses make the verse by little and little: The witnesses note the put out by little and little: the gradual conquest. The witnesses note the not consume at once: lest the land lie empty. The witnesses note the lest the beasts increase: a danger forestalled. The witnesses note the God's wise gradual working: deliverance by degrees, fitted to their strength. The expositors observe the put out by little and little: the gradual conquest. The expositors observe the not consume at once: lest the land lie empty. The expositors observe the lest the beasts increase: a danger forestalled. The expositors observe the God's wise gradual working: deliverance by degrees, fitted to their strength. The believer is taught to mark the put out by little and little, the not consume at once, the lest the beasts increase, the God's wise gradual working, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that God would drive out the nations by little and little, lest the beasts increase — the gradual conquest an act of wisdom and care, God's deliverances coming by degrees, fitted to his people's strength.
23:
"But the LORD thy God shall deliver them unto thee, and shall destroy them with a mighty destruction, until they be destroyed." The witnesses note that, gradual though it was, the destruction of the nations would be complete and mighty. The witnesses note that Gill notes God would deliver them gradually, by little and little, until at length they should all come into Israel's hands and be destroyed. The witnesses note that it would be a mighty destruction, until they be destroyed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the shall deliver them — gradually into their hands; the a mighty destruction — complete and overwhelming; the until they be destroyed — the work brought to its end; the gradual yet certain — the patience of God's method, the certainty of his purpose.

The witnesses agree the gradual working would yet reach a sure and total end: though God drove out the nations little by little, he would in the end "destroy them with a mighty destruction, until they be destroyed," the slowness not lessening the completeness; the verse balances the former, that the patience of God's method takes nothing from the certainty of his purpose — what he begins by degrees he finishes wholly, the deliverance sure though gradual, the work certain to its appointed close.

So the witnesses make the verse a mighty destruction: The witnesses note the shall deliver them: gradually into their hands. The witnesses note the a mighty destruction: complete and overwhelming. The witnesses note the until they be destroyed: the work brought to its end. The witnesses note the gradual yet certain: the patience of God's method, the certainty of his purpose. The expositors observe the shall deliver them: gradually into their hands. The expositors observe the a mighty destruction: complete and overwhelming. The expositors observe the until they be destroyed: the work brought to its end. The expositors observe the gradual yet certain: the patience of God's method, the certainty of his purpose. The believer is taught to mark the shall deliver them, the a mighty destruction, the until they be destroyed, the gradual yet certain, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that God would destroy the nations with a mighty destruction, gradual yet complete — the patience of his method taking nothing from the certainty of his purpose, the work sure to its appointed close.
24:
"And he shall deliver their kings into thine hand, and thou shalt destroy their name from under heaven: there shall no man be able to stand before thee, until thou have destroyed them." The witnesses note God delivering the many kings of Canaan, none able to stand before Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes the kings were very numerous — though seven nations, there were one and thirty kings. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the summary of Joshua's conquest reckons the kings for the cities. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the their kings into thine hand — the one-and-thirty kings; the destroy their name — blotted from under heaven; the none able to stand — before God's people; the irresistible help — no power withstanding him (Josh. 1:5).

The witnesses agree the promise of total victory extends even to the many kings: God would deliver the one-and-thirty kings into Israel's hand, blot out their name, and let none stand before them till all were destroyed; the sweeping pledge — "no man be able to stand before thee" — is the assurance of God's irresistible help, that with him going before, no power however royal or many can withstand his people, the victory not partial but entire, the very names of the enemy blotted out (cf. Josh. 1:5).

So the witnesses make the verse kings delivered: The witnesses note the their kings into thine hand: the one-and-thirty kings. The witnesses note the destroy their name: blotted from under heaven. The witnesses note the none able to stand: before God's people. The witnesses note the irresistible help: no power withstanding him (Josh. 1:5). The expositors observe the their kings into thine hand: the one-and-thirty kings. The expositors observe the destroy their name: blotted from under heaven. The expositors observe the none able to stand: before God's people. The expositors observe the irresistible help: no power withstanding him (Josh. 1:5). The believer is taught to mark the their kings into thine hand, the destroy their name, the none able to stand, the irresistible help, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that God would deliver the kings of Canaan and let none stand before Israel — the assurance of his irresistible help, no power however royal withstanding his people, the victory entire (Josh. 1:5).
25:
"The graven images of their gods shall ye burn with fire: thou shalt not desire the silver or gold that is on them... lest thou be snared therein: for it is an abomination to the LORD thy God." The witnesses note the charge to burn the idols and not even covet the silver and gold on them, lest they be snared. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is repeated that it might be the more strictly performed; unless done, they could not expect the destruction of their enemies. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the burning of the graven images. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the burn the graven images — the idols destroyed; the desire not the silver or gold — not even the precious metal; the lest thou be snared — covetousness a subtler trap; the an abomination — the spoils of sin to be shunned (Josh. 7:21).

The witnesses agree the command guards against a subtler snare — covetousness: not only were the idols to be burned, but the very gold and silver upon them not to be desired, lest the precious metal of an idol catch the heart that despised the idol itself; the warning reaches past open idolatry to the greed that would profit by it, teaching that the people of God must keep clear even of the spoils of sin, the love of money a snare as deadly as the idol — a lesson written large in Achan's sin (cf. Josh. 7:21).

So the witnesses make the verse covet not the gold: The witnesses note the burn the graven images: the idols destroyed. The witnesses note the desire not the silver or gold: not even the precious metal. The witnesses note the lest thou be snared: covetousness a subtler trap. The witnesses note the an abomination: the spoils of sin to be shunned (Josh. 7:21). The expositors observe the burn the graven images: the idols destroyed. The expositors observe the desire not the silver or gold: not even the precious metal. The expositors observe the lest thou be snared: covetousness a subtler trap. The expositors observe the an abomination: the spoils of sin to be shunned (Josh. 7:21). The believer is taught to mark the burn the graven images, the desire not the silver or gold, the lest thou be snared, the an abomination, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that the idols were to be burned and their gold not even desired, lest it snare — the warning against the covetousness that would profit by sin, the love of money a snare as deadly as the idol (Josh. 7:21).
26:
"Neither shalt thou bring an abomination into thine house, lest thou be a cursed thing like it: but thou shalt utterly detest it... for it is a cursed thing." The witnesses note the charge not to bring any idolatrous abomination home, lest the man become accursed like it. The witnesses note that Gill notes the abomination is an idol, with anything appertaining to its service; this care was taken to prevent idolatry and all appearance of it. The witnesses note that the man bringing it would become a cursed thing like it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bring no abomination home — no relic of the idol; the lest thou be cursed like it — the curse cleaving to the man; the utterly detest it — an entire abhorrence; the separate from it — touch not the unclean thing (2 Cor. 6:17).

The witnesses agree the final charge presses an utter abhorrence of idolatry: not the least relic of it was to be brought into the house, lest the man himself become "a cursed thing" like the idol he harboured; the principle is that contact with the accursed brings the curse, so that the people of God must wholly detest and put away every trace of false worship, separating themselves utterly from the unclean thing — a word the apostle takes up, "come out from among them... and touch not the unclean thing" (2 Cor. 6:17).

So the witnesses make the verse the cursed thing: The witnesses note the bring no abomination home: no relic of the idol. The witnesses note the lest thou be cursed like it: the curse cleaving to the man. The witnesses note the utterly detest it: an entire abhorrence. The witnesses note the separate from it: touch not the unclean thing (2 Cor. 6:17). The expositors observe the bring no abomination home: no relic of the idol. The expositors observe the lest thou be cursed like it: the curse cleaving to the man. The expositors observe the utterly detest it: an entire abhorrence. The expositors observe the separate from it: touch not the unclean thing (2 Cor. 6:17). The believer is taught to mark the bring no abomination home, the lest thou be cursed like it, the utterly detest it, the separate from it, and not to fear, for God is in the midst. The verse thus records that no idolatrous abomination was to be brought home, lest the man be accursed like it — an utter abhorrence of idolatry, the people of God separating wholly from the unclean thing (2 Cor. 6:17).
